question: What is Amazon Bedrock, what are foundation models, and how do Bedrock APIs work?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed, serverless service for building generative AI applications using foundation models

KEY POINT:
- No infrastructure management required
- Unified API across multiple models

---

FOUNDATION MODELS (FM):
- Large pre-trained models trained on massive datasets
- Used for:
  - Text generation
  - Image generation
  - Embeddings

EXAMPLES:
- Amazon Titan
- Anthropic Claude
- Meta (Llama)

NOTE:
- OpenAI models are typically NOT part of Bedrock

KEY IDEA:
- Foundation models = base models you build applications on top of

---

BEDROCK AS A PLATFORM:
- Acts as a model marketplace + unified API
- Supports multiple providers through a single interface
- Enables switching models without rewriting code

---

CORE CAPABILITIES:
- Text generation
- Chat applications
- Embeddings
- Image generation
- Fine-tuning
- Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
- Agents (tool-based workflows)

---

SERVERLESS BENEFIT:
- No need to manage GPUs or infrastructure
- Automatic scaling
- Pay-per-use model

---

BEDROCK APIs (VERY IMPORTANT):

1. Bedrock API (Control Plane)
- Model management
- Training and fine-tuning
- Setup before production

2. Bedrock Runtime (Data Plane)
- Real-time inference

Key operations:
- invoke_model
- converse
- streaming variants available

3. Bedrock Agent API
- Create and manage agents and knowledge bases

4. Bedrock Agent Runtime
- Execute agents and RAG workflows

Key operations:
- invoke_agent
- retrieve
- retrieve_and_generate

---

STREAMING (IMPORTANT):
- Output returned token-by-token (like ChatGPT typing)
- Improves user experience for long responses

---

ACCESS CONTROL (EXAM CRITICAL):
- Must use IAM user or role
- Root account usage is NOT supported for Bedrock operations

Required permissions:
- AmazonBedrockFullAccess (or equivalent)

---

MODEL ACCESS REQUIREMENT:
- Must request access before using a model
- Accept EULA (End User License Agreement)

Behavior:
- Amazon Titan → immediate access
- Third-party models → quick approval

IMPORTANT:
- Pricing is NOT clearly shown during access request

---

PRICING MODEL:
- Pay-per-use
- Pricing set by model provider
- Billed through AWS

---

PLAYGROUNDS:
- Chat playground
- Text generation playground
- Image generation playground

Used for:
- Testing prompts
- Evaluating models
- Experimentation

---

SAGEMAKER RELATIONSHIP:
- Bedrock → fast GenAI development
- SageMaker → full ML control

Bedrock can integrate with SageMaker workflows

---

EXAM TRAPS:
- Thinking Bedrock includes OpenAI models → WRONG
- Thinking Bedrock requires infrastructure setup → WRONG
- Using root account → WRONG
- Assuming all models are enabled by default → WRONG

---

SUMMARY:
- Amazon Bedrock is a serverless GenAI platform
- Provides unified access to multiple foundation models
- Supports inference, agents, RAG, and model management

KEY IDEA:
- “Fastest way to build GenAI applications on AWS using foundation models”

question: How do Amazon Bedrock playground modes and inference parameters (temperature, top-k, top-p, system prompt) affect model behavior?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- Bedrock playground allows you to experiment with foundation models
- You control behavior using inference parameters

---

PLAYGROUND MODES:

1. Chat Mode
- Multi-turn conversation
- Maintains context/history
- Responses depend on previous messages

2. Single Prompt Mode
- One-shot request → response
- No memory/context

KEY DIFFERENCE:
- Chat = contextual
- Single prompt = stateless

---

SYSTEM PROMPT (VERY IMPORTANT):
- Defines global behavior/persona
- Applies to entire interaction

Example:
- "Respond like a pirate"

KEY IDEA:
- System prompt controls tone, role, and behavior

---

HOW MODELS GENERATE TEXT:
- Predict next token repeatedly
- Each step selects from candidate tokens based on probability distribution

---

RANDOMNESS CONTROLS (EXAM CRITICAL):

Temperature:
- Controls randomness of output
- Low (0) → deterministic (safe, predictable)
- High → creative, diverse responses  [oai_citation:0‡AWS Documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/inference-parameters.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Top-K:
- Limits number of candidate tokens
- Example: K=50 → choose from top 50 tokens  [oai_citation:1‡AWS Documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cdk/api/v2/python/aws_cdk.aws_bedrock/CfnAgent.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Top-P (Nucleus Sampling):
- Selects tokens based on probability threshold
- Example: P=0.8 → choose from top 80% probability mass  [oai_citation:2‡AWS Documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/APIReference/API_runtime_InferenceConfiguration.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

KEY DIFFERENCE:
- Top-K → fixed number of options
- Top-P → probability-based filtering

---

OUTPUT CONTROL:

Max Tokens:
- Limits response length

Stop Sequences:
- Define when generation stops

---

MODEL REASONING:
- Breaks complex problems into steps (chain-of-thought style)
- Improves accuracy for complex tasks
- Tradeoff:
  - Higher cost
  - Higher latency

---

MULTIMODAL CAPABILITIES:
- Supports:
  - Text
  - Documents
  - Images

Use cases:
- Document Q&A
- Context-aware responses

---

IMAGE PLAYGROUND:

Capabilities:
- Generate images
- Modify images:
  - remove object
  - replace background
  - variations

Controls:
- Prompt / negative prompt
- Style
- Seed (reproducibility)
- Strength (creativity vs adherence)

---

SEED (IMPORTANT):
- Same seed → same output
- Used for reproducibility

---

GUARDRAILS (HIGH LEVEL):
- Prevent:
  - unsafe content
  - PII leakage
  - policy violations

---

EXAM TRAPS:
- Chat and single prompt are the same → WRONG
- Temperature controls number of tokens → WRONG
- Top-K = probability threshold → WRONG
- System prompt only affects first response → WRONG

---

SUMMARY:
- Bedrock playground lets you control:
  - interaction mode (chat vs single prompt)
  - behavior (system prompt)
  - randomness (temperature, top-k, top-p)
  - output (tokens, stop)

KEY IDEA:
- “You control how the model behaves through inference parameters”

question: What is fine-tuning in Amazon Bedrock, how does it differ from continued pre-training, and when should you use each?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- Fine-tuning = extending a foundation model with your own data
- Result = a custom model with new knowledge/behavior baked in

---

FINE-TUNING (CUSTOM MODELS):

Definition:
- Train a foundation model on labeled examples
- Uses prompt → completion pairs

Format:
{
  "prompt": "...",
  "completion": "..."
}

---

WHAT IT DOES:

- Learns:
  - style
  - tone
  - behavior
  - task-specific patterns

Examples:
- Brand voice chatbot
- Customer support assistant
- Classification tasks
- Ad copy generation

---

KEY BENEFITS:

1. KNOWLEDGE IS BAKED IN
- No need to repeat context in prompts

2. LOWER TOKEN COST (LONG TERM)
- Smaller prompts
- Less repetition

3. CONSISTENT OUTPUT
- Stable tone/personality

---

TRADEOFFS:

- Expensive upfront (training cost)
- Time-consuming
- Requires dataset preparation

---

CONTINUED PRE-TRAINING:

Definition:
- Train model on UNLABELED data
- No prompt/completion pairs

Input:
- Raw text (documents, logs, etc.)

---

KEY DIFFERENCE:

Fine-tuning:
- Labeled data (Q → A)
- Teaches behavior/style

Continued pre-training:
- Unlabeled data
- Expands knowledge

---

WHEN TO USE:

Fine-tuning:
- You want:
  - specific tone/personality
  - structured outputs
  - task behavior

Continued pre-training:
- You want:
  - domain knowledge
  - more data coverage

---

EXAMPLES:

Fine-tuning:
→ Train model to sound like your company brand

Continued pre-training:
→ Train model on internal documents

---

SUPPORTED MODELS (IMPORTANT):

- Not all Bedrock models support fine-tuning
- Examples:
  - Titan
  - Cohere
  - Meta

Always check model capabilities

---

IMAGE MODEL FINE-TUNING:

- Input:
  - image + description pairs
- Use cases:
  - style transfer
  - brand visuals
  - consistent image generation

---

SECURITY (EXAM TRAP):

Sensitive data:
→ Use VPC + PrivateLink

KEY IDEA:
- Keep training data private and secure

---

COST INSIGHT:

Short term:
→ Expensive (training)

Long term:
→ Cheaper (less prompt tokens)

---

ALTERNATIVES (IMPORTANT CONTEXT):

Instead of fine-tuning:
- Prompt engineering
- RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)

WHY:
- Faster
- cheaper
- more flexible

---

EXAM TRAPS:

- Fine-tuning = prompt engineering → WRONG
- Continued pre-training uses labels → WRONG
- All models support fine-tuning → WRONG
- Fine-tuning is always cheaper → WRONG (only long-term)

---

SUMMARY:

Fine-tuning:
→ teaches HOW to respond

Continued pre-training:
→ teaches WHAT to know

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Fine-tuning = behavior, Pre-training = knowledge”

question: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in Amazon Bedrock, how does it work, and when should it be used instead of fine-tuning?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- RAG = combine search + LLM
- Instead of training the model → inject external data into the prompt

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Don’t teach the model — give it the answer at runtime”

---

HOW RAG WORKS (STEP-BY-STEP):

1. User prompt
→ "What did the president say yesterday?"

2. Retrieve data
→ Search external knowledge base (documents, DB, vector store)

3. Augment prompt
→ Add retrieved content to prompt

4. Send to LLM
→ LLM generates answer using:
   - retrieved data
   - its own knowledge

---

FINAL PROMPT STRUCTURE:

Original:
→ "What did the president say yesterday?"

Augmented:
→ "Answer using this context:
   [retrieved document]
   Question: ..."

---

BEDROCK TERMINOLOGY:

RAG = Knowledge Base

- Retrieval = Bedrock Agent → retrieve
- Generation = Bedrock Runtime → generate

---

KEY COMPONENTS:

1. Embeddings
- Convert text → vectors

2. Vector Database
- Stores embeddings
- Enables semantic search

3. Retriever
- Finds relevant chunks

4. Generator (LLM)
- Produces final answer

---

SEMANTIC SEARCH (IMPORTANT):

- Not keyword search
- Finds meaning similarity

Example:
"car" ≈ "vehicle"

---

ADVANTAGES OF RAG:

1. NO RETRAINING NEEDED
- Just update database

2. FASTER & CHEAPER (SHORT TERM)
- No expensive training jobs

3. ALWAYS UP-TO-DATE
- Add new documents easily

4. USES PROPRIETARY DATA
- Without modifying model

---

LIMITATIONS:

1. MORE TOKENS (COST)
- Larger prompts

2. RELIES ON RETRIEVAL QUALITY
- Bad data → bad answers

3. CAN STILL HALLUCINATE

4. COMPLEX PIPELINE
- Not just “LLM call”

---

BIGGEST REAL-WORLD PROBLEM:

👉 Retrieval quality

If retrieved content is:
- irrelevant
- incomplete
- poorly chunked

→ Output quality collapses

---

RAG vs FINE-TUNING:

RAG:
- Inject knowledge at runtime
- Dynamic
- Cheaper (short-term)

Fine-tuning:
- Bake knowledge into model
- Static
- Expensive upfront

---

WHEN TO USE RAG:

- Frequently changing data
- Large document sets
- Internal/company knowledge
- Search + QA systems

---

WHEN NOT IDEAL:

- Need strict tone/style control
- Need deterministic outputs
- Retrieval quality is poor

---

EXAM TRAPS:

- RAG = training model → ❌
- RAG prevents hallucination completely → ❌
- RAG replaces LLM → ❌
- RAG always cheaper → ❌ (token cost adds up)

---

MENTAL MODEL:

RAG = "Open-book exam"
Fine-tuning = "Memorized knowledge"

---

SUMMARY:

RAG:
→ Retrieve → Augment → Generate

KEY IDEA:
👉 “LLM + Search = Practical AI system”

question: How do embeddings and vector databases enable retrieval in RAG systems, and why are they critical to output quality?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- RAG depends on retrieval quality
- Retrieval quality depends on embeddings + vector search

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Better vectors → better retrieval → better answers”

---

WHAT IS AN EMBEDDING?

- A vector representation of data
- Encodes meaning (semantics)

Example:
"text" → [0.12, -0.44, 0.98, ...]

---

KEY PROPERTY:

- Similar meaning → close in vector space

Examples:
- "car" ≈ "vehicle"
- "potato" ≈ "rhubarb"

---

WHAT IS A VECTOR DATABASE?

Stores:
- Original data (text/images)
- Embeddings (vectors)

Purpose:
→ Enable semantic search

---

HOW RETRIEVAL WORKS:

1. Convert query → embedding vector
2. Search vector DB
3. Find closest vectors (k-nearest neighbors)
4. Return top-k relevant documents

---

DISTANCE METRICS:

- Cosine similarity (most common)
- Measures:
  → how close vectors are

---

IMPORTANT STEP:

Query → embedding → similarity search

NOT:
Query → keyword match

---

WHY THIS MATTERS:

Traditional search:
- Keyword-based
- brittle

Vector search:
- Meaning-based
- robust

---

VECTOR SEARCH PROCESS:

Prompt:
→ "Tell me about Data’s daughter"

System:
→ Converts to embedding
→ Finds closest vectors

Returns:
→ Relevant text chunks

---

CRITICAL FACTOR:

👉 Retrieval quality drives output quality

If retrieved data is:
- irrelevant → bad output
- incomplete → weak output
- noisy → hallucinations

---

BIGGEST FAILURE MODE:

Chunking

- Documents split into chunks
- Poor chunking → meaningless fragments

Example:
- sentence split mid-thought → useless context

---

KEY TUNING PARAMETERS:

1. K (top results)
- Too low → missing context
- Too high → noisy context

2. Chunk size
- Too small → fragmented meaning
- Too large → irrelevant info

3. Embedding model
- Quality affects retrieval

---

DATABASE OPTIONS:

1. Vector-native:
- Pinecone
- Weaviate
- Chroma

2. Extended DBs:
- OpenSearch (common in AWS)
- MongoDB
- Redis
- Cassandra

---

AWS CONTEXT:

- Bedrock Knowledge Base → uses vector DB
- OpenSearch commonly used

---

EMBEDDINGS SOURCE:

- Generated via models:
  - Titan Embeddings
  - Cohere
  - others

---

IMPORTANT INSIGHT:

👉 Transformers already use embeddings internally

RAG:
→ reuses that capability externally

---

HYBRID SEARCH (ADVANCED):

Combine:
- Keyword search (TF-IDF)
- Vector search

Result:
→ better retrieval accuracy

---

EXAM TRAPS:

- Vector DB = keyword search → ❌
- Embeddings are low-dimensional → ❌ (often 100s–1000s)
- RAG works without embeddings → ❌
- Retrieval quality doesn’t matter → ❌ (most critical factor)

---

MENTAL MODEL:

Embedding space = map of meaning

Query:
→ “Where is this idea located?”

Search:
→ “Find nearby ideas”

---

SUMMARY:

RAG retrieval pipeline:

Query → Embedding → Vector Search → Top-K Results → Prompt Augmentation

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Garbage retrieval = garbage generation”

question: How do Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases implement RAG, and what are the key components and design considerations?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- Bedrock Knowledge Base = managed RAG system
- Handles ingestion → embedding → storage → retrieval

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Bedrock automates RAG pipeline end-to-end”

---

DATA SOURCES (INGESTION):

Supported inputs:
- S3 (structured + unstructured)
- Web crawler
- Confluence
- Salesforce
- SharePoint

---

PIPELINE OVERVIEW:

1. Ingest documents
2. Chunk documents
3. Generate embeddings
4. Store in vector DB
5. Retrieve during query
6. Augment prompt
7. Generate response

---

EMBEDDING STEP:

- Uses foundation models:
  - Amazon Titan Embeddings
  - Cohere

Configurable:
- Vector dimension

---

VECTOR STORE OPTIONS:

Default:
- OpenSearch (most common)

Other options:
- Aurora
- MongoDB Atlas
- Pinecone
- Redis
- MemoryDB

---

CHUNKING (CRITICAL):

Purpose:
→ Break large documents into smaller pieces

Default:
→ ~300 characters (basic)

Better approaches:
- Sentence-based
- Paragraph-based
- Semantic chunking

---

WHY CHUNKING MATTERS:

Bad chunking:
→ broken meaning
→ poor retrieval
→ bad output

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Chunks must represent complete ideas”

---

CHUNK CONFIGURATION:

You can control:
- Chunk size
- Overlap between chunks

Tradeoff:
- Small chunks → precise but fragmented
- Large chunks → rich but noisy

---

RETRIEVAL FLOW:

1. User query
2. Convert → embedding
3. Search vector DB
4. Retrieve top-K chunks
5. Combine into context
6. Send to LLM

---

PROMPT AUGMENTATION:

Final prompt:

"Answer using this context:
[retrieved chunks]
Question: ..."

---

BEDROCK EXECUTION FLOW:

User Query
→ Embedding
→ OpenSearch (vector search)
→ Retrieved context
→ LLM (generation)
→ Final response

---

WAYS TO USE KNOWLEDGE BASE:

1. Console:
- “Chat with your document”

2. API:
- integrate into apps

3. Agents:
- Agentic RAG (advanced)

---

KEY ADVANTAGE:

- No need for fine-tuning
- Just update data source

---

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS:

1. Chunking strategy (MOST IMPORTANT)
2. Embedding quality
3. Vector DB choice
4. Retrieval quality (top-K)
5. Prompt template

---

COMMON FAILURE POINTS:

- Arbitrary chunking (e.g. fixed 300 chars)
- Irrelevant retrieval
- Too many/too few results
- Poor data structure

---

BETTER ALTERNATIVES (ADVANCED):

Instead of raw chunking:
- Structured data
- Knowledge graphs
- Hybrid search

---

EXAM TRAPS:

- Knowledge Base = fine-tuning → ❌
- OpenSearch is mandatory → ❌
- Chunking doesn’t matter → ❌
- RAG doesn’t use embeddings → ❌

---

MENTAL MODEL:

Bedrock KB =

Documents → Chunks → Embeddings → Vector DB → Retrieval → Prompt → LLM

---

SUMMARY:

Bedrock Knowledge Base:

→ Fully managed RAG system

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Quality of chunks + retrieval = quality of AI output”

question: What are Amazon Bedrock Guardrails, and how do they enforce AI safety, prevent misuse, and reduce hallucinations?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- Guardrails = safety + governance layer for GenAI
- Applies to both inputs (prompts) and outputs (responses)

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Control what goes in AND what comes out of the model”

---

SCOPE:

Applies to:
- Prompts (user input)
- Responses (model output)

Works with:
- Text foundation models (Titan, Claude)

NOT (currently):
- Image models

---

CONTENT FILTERING:

1. WORD-LEVEL FILTERING
- Block specific keywords
- Example:
  → competitor names
  → banned phrases

---

2. TOPIC-LEVEL FILTERING
- Block entire topics

Examples:
- politics
- religion
- sensitive domains

---

3. SAFETY CATEGORIES (BUILT-IN)

Automatically detect:
- Hate speech
- Bias
- Harmful content
- Unsafe topics

---

4. PROFANITY FILTER

- Built-in bad word detection
- No manual setup required

---

PII PROTECTION:

Detects and handles:
- Phone numbers
- Addresses
- IDs (SSN, etc.)

Options:
- Remove
- Mask (e.g. [ADDRESS])

---

KEY IDEA:
👉 Prevents data leakage + compliance issues

---

CONTEXTUAL GROUNDING CHECK (ADVANCED):

Purpose:
→ Reduce hallucinations

---

METRIC 1: GROUNDING

Measures:
→ How well response aligns with retrieved context

Low grounding:
→ model is making things up

---

METRIC 2: RELEVANCE

Measures:
→ How well response answers the question

Low relevance:
→ off-topic answer

---

CONTROL:

- Set thresholds
- If below threshold → response blocked

---

IMPORTANT LIMITATION:

👉 Works only as well as your RAG quality

If retrieval is bad:
→ grounding check becomes weak

---

WHERE USED:

- Knowledge Bases (RAG)
- Agents
- Any Bedrock application

---

BLOCKED RESPONSE HANDLING:

- Custom message can be defined

Example:
"This request cannot be processed due to policy restrictions"

---

KEY USE CASES:

- Prevent harmful outputs
- Protect sensitive data (PII)
- Enforce company policy
- Reduce hallucinations (partially)

---

DESIGN PATTERN:

User Input
→ Guardrail (input filter)
→ Model
→ Guardrail (output filter)
→ User

---

EXAM TRAPS:

- Guardrails only filter output → ❌ (input + output)
- Prevent hallucinations completely → ❌ (partial only)
- Work without RAG → ❌ (grounding depends on context)
- Only keyword-based → ❌ (also semantic + safety)

---

MENTAL MODEL:

Guardrails = AI firewall

- Before model → filter input
- After model → filter output

---

SUMMARY:

Bedrock Guardrails:

→ Content filtering + PII protection + hallucination control

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Safety layer around GenAI systems”

question: How do you configure and test Amazon Bedrock Guardrails in practice, and what key controls are available?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- Guardrails are configurable safety policies
- Applied via UI or API before deployment

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Define → Configure → Test → Deploy”

---

SETUP FLOW:

1. Create Guardrail
2. Define filters
3. Configure thresholds
4. Test with prompts
5. Deploy to agents / knowledge bases

---

STEP 1: BASIC CONFIG

- Name + description
- Default blocked message

Example:
"Sorry, this request cannot be processed"

---

OPTIONAL:

- Custom responses per violation type
- Cross-region inference (for scale + newer features)
- KMS key (encryption)

---

STEP 2: CONTENT FILTERS

A. HARMFUL CATEGORIES

- Hate
- Insults
- Violence
- Toxicity

Control:
- Threshold (Low / Medium / High)

---

KEY IDEA:
👉 Higher threshold = stricter filtering

---

B. PROMPT ATTACK PROTECTION

Detects:
- “Ignore previous instructions”
- jailbreak attempts

Options:
- Block
- Detect only

---

RISK:
- Can produce false positives

---

C. TOPIC FILTERS (CUSTOM)

Example:
- Block politics

Define:
- Description
- Example prompts

Controls:
- Block input
- Block output

---

KEY IDEA:
👉 Needs clear definitions + examples to work well

---

D. WORD FILTERS

- Built-in profanity filter
- Custom word blocking

Example:
- Block "potato"

Applies to:
- Input
- Output

---

E. PII FILTERING

Detects:
- SSN
- phone numbers
- addresses

Actions:
- Mask → [PHONE_NUMBER]
- Remove
- Block

---

STEP 3: CONTEXTUAL CHECKS

A. GROUNDING

- Matches response vs retrieved context

Low score:
→ hallucination risk

---

B. RELEVANCE

- Matches response vs user query

Low score:
→ off-topic answer

---

Control:
- Threshold + block/detect

---

LIMITATION:

👉 Depends on RAG quality

---

STEP 4: TESTING

Use test console:

Try:
- banned words
- blocked topics
- prompt attacks

---

OUTPUT:

- Response blocked
- Trace available

Trace shows:
- which rule triggered
- why it triggered

---

KEY IDEA:
👉 Trace = debugging tool for guardrails

---

STEP 5: DEPLOYMENT

Attach guardrail to:
- Knowledge Base (RAG)
- Agents
- Applications

---

RUNTIME FLOW:

User Input
→ Guardrail (input)
→ Model
→ Guardrail (output)
→ Response

---

REAL EXAMPLES:

Input:
"I like potato"

→ Blocked (word filter)

---

Input:
"Should I vote Democrat?"

→ Blocked (topic filter)

---

TRACE OUTPUT:

- word_filter triggered
- topic_filter triggered

---

ADVANCED OPTIONS:

- Multimodal filtering (text + images)
- Automated reasoning checks (detect-only)

---

DESIGN INSIGHT:

👉 Guardrails are policy engines, not intelligence

They:
- enforce rules
- do not improve model quality

---

COMMON MISTAKES:

- Over-blocking → poor UX
- Under-defining topics → weak filtering
- Ignoring false positives
- Not testing edge cases

---

EXAM TRAPS:

- Guardrails require code → ❌ (UI supported)
- Only keyword filtering → ❌ (semantic + category)
- No debugging → ❌ (trace available)
- Only input filtering → ❌ (input + output)

---

MENTAL MODEL:

Guardrails = configurable policy layer

- Define what is allowed
- Enforce consistently
- Monitor with trace

---

SUMMARY:

Guardrails workflow:

Define rules → Apply filters → Test → Deploy

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Control behavior before production, not after problems”

question: What advanced capabilities does Amazon Bedrock provide beyond basic model usage, including model import, evaluation, throughput control, and collaboration?
answer:
CORE IDEA:
- Bedrock is not just model access
- It provides evaluation, customization, scaling, and collaboration tools

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Bedrock = platform for building, evaluating, and operating GenAI”

---

1. MODEL IMPORT (BYOM)

Capability:
- Bring Your Own Model (BYOM)

Sources:
- SageMaker-trained models
- Models stored in S3

---

USE CASE:

- Custom foundation models
- Proprietary LLMs
- Specialized domain models

---

KEY IDEA:
👉 Not limited to Bedrock marketplace models

---

2. MODEL EVALUATION

Challenge:
- GenAI outputs are non-deterministic
- Hard to measure “correctness”

---

EVALUATION TYPES:

A. AUTOMATED METRICS

- Accuracy
- Toxicity
- Robustness
- F1 Score
- BERTScore

---

HOW IT WORKS:

- Provide test dataset:
  - prompts
  - expected responses

System:
→ compares outputs vs expected

---

B. HUMAN EVALUATION

- Humans rank responses

Example:
- Compare multiple outputs
- Choose best one

---

METHOD:

- Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)

---

OPTIONS:

- Bring your own team
- Use AWS managed workforce (like Mechanical Turk)

---

KEY IDEA:
👉 Human judgment is still critical in GenAI evaluation

---

3. PROVISIONED THROUGHPUT

Purpose:
- Guarantee capacity for inference

---

OPTIONS:

1. On-Demand
- Shared capacity
- Default

2. Provisioned
- Reserved capacity
- Higher cost
- More predictable performance

---

USE WHEN:

- High traffic
- Production workloads
- Latency-sensitive apps

---

4. WATERMARK DETECTION

Applies to:
- Titan Image Generation

---

FEATURE:

- Generated images contain embedded watermark
- Bedrock can detect it

---

USE CASE:

- Identify AI-generated content
- Content authenticity validation

---

5. BEDROCK STUDIO

What it is:
- Web-based development environment

---

KEY FEATURES:

- No AWS account required per user
- Uses IAM + SSO integration
- Team collaboration

---

USE CASES:

- Enterprise environments
- Shared AI development workspace
- Secure access without full AWS accounts

---

KEY IDEA:
👉 Enables controlled access + collaboration at scale

---

COLLABORATION FEATURES:

- Shared projects
- Multiple users
- Centralized access control

---

6. BUILT-IN DATASETS (EVALUATION)

- Predefined prompt datasets
- Used for benchmarking models

---

USE CASE:

- Compare models quickly
- Standardized evaluation

---

MENTAL MODEL:

Bedrock layers:

1. Models (FM / custom / imported)
2. Evaluation (auto + human)
3. Scaling (throughput)
4. Safety (guardrails)
5. Collaboration (Studio)

---

EXAM TRAPS:

- Bedrock only uses AWS models → ❌ (supports third-party + BYOM)
- Evaluation is fully automated → ❌ (human eval critical)
- Provisioned throughput = cheaper → ❌ (more expensive but stable)
- Bedrock requires AWS account per user → ❌ (Studio avoids this)
- Watermark applies to all models → ❌ (Titan image only)

---

SUMMARY:

Bedrock advanced features include:

- Model import (BYOM)
- Model evaluation (auto + human)
- Throughput control (on-demand vs provisioned)
- Watermark detection
- Bedrock Studio (collaboration)

KEY IDEA:
👉 “Bedrock is a full GenAI platform, not just an API”
